content.problemStatement:
​A `1`-indexed array is called positive if every element of the array is greater than or equal to the index on which it lies. Formally, an array BBB of size MMM is called positive if Bi≥iB_i \ge iBi​≥i for each 1≤i≤M1\le i \le M1≤i≤M.

For example, the arrays [1],[2,2],[3,2,4,4][1], [2, 2], [3, 2, 4, 4][1],[2,2],[3,2,4,4] are positive while the arrays [2,1],[3,1,2][2, 1], [3, 1, 2][2,1],[3,1,2] are not positive.

You are given an array AAA containing NNN integers. You want to distribute all elements of the array into some positive arrays. The elements of a positive array might not occur in the order they appear in the array AAA.

Find the **minimum** number of positive arrays that the elements of the array AAA can be divided into.

Please see the sample cases below for more clarity.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the number of elements in the array AAA.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2,\ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of positive arrays that the elements of the array AAA can be divided into.
​
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible number of positive arrays into which all elements can be divided.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The greedy algorithm described in the approach yields exactly the numbers shown in the original statement.
A single element array is always positive, and five copies of 5 can form one positive array of size 5.
Each positive array can contain at most two elements (because the third position would need a value ≥3). Hence the optimal division is two arrays of size 2.

solution.approach: Sort the whole array in non‑decreasing order. Scan the sorted values while building the current positive array. Let cur be the number of elements already placed in the current array. For each value x: increase cur by one (we try to put x at position cur). If cur > x the condition B_cur >= cur would be violated, therefore the current array must be closed. Increment the answer (a finished array), start a new array with the current element (cur = 1). After processing all numbers, if cur > 0 add one more to the answer. This greedy construction is optimal because in a sorted order the smallest remaining element is the most restrictive; delaying its placement can never reduce the number of arrays.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting (overall O((ΣN) log N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the array for each test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort()
        groups = 0
        cur = 0
        for x in arr:
            cur += 1
            if cur > x:
                groups += 1
                cur = 1
        if cur > 0:
            groups += 1
        out_lines.append(str(groups))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
